evidence_quotes.quote: This has already led to Visa and Mastercard censoring video games on both Steam and Itch.io, simply because they did not approve of the content shown within these games.
Currently, when you buy a video game, you do not actually buy the game itself, but only a license.
I believe that video games should not be allowed to be sold as licenses, but as the actual products that they are.

paragraphs.text: There are two points of feedback which I wish would be added to the Digital Fairness Act, those being the following: The first is that payment providers, such as Visa and Mastercard, must not be allowed to pick and choose what (digital) products their services are used for. They more or less have monopolies on these services, with there being very few other options available for anyone who wishes to buy or sell anything online. This means that, should these payment providers decide that they do not wish to provide their services to such a product, there are no real alternatives available. This has already led to Visa and Mastercard censoring video games on both Steam and Itch.io, simply because they did not approve of the content shown within these games. Games that, by all laws and other regulations, are completely legal. Steam and itch.io were forced to remove or otherwise hide the games that Visa and Mastercard did not approve of. It is my opinion that these payment service provides should not be allowed this amount of power, as they are monopolies in the market and as such have an important role to fulfill. A role that comes with certain responsibilities as well. While it may start with censoring certain types of video games, there is nothing stopping them from exercising this power on other products as well, should they not be made to stop here. As such, I hope a clause will be added to the Digital Fairness Act, wherein it is stated that payment service providers with such monopolies must allow any and all types of products to be sold using their services, as long as these products are otherwise of legal status and do not break any existing laws. My second point, is that attention should be given to video game publisher's ability to destroy their own games. Currently, when you buy a video game, you do not actually buy the game itself, but only a license. This means that, should the publishers of the game choose to have the game's life cycle end, the publishers and revoke a person's access to a game they have bought, stopping you from playing it ever again. This does not just pertain to games that have need of online services to be played, such as multiplayer games where multiple people play on the same server, but also to singleplayer games which are completely playable without depending on any online services. Sometimes, even owning a physical copy of a video game, such as a DVD, is not enough to allow you to keep access. It is my believe that video games should not be allowed to be sold as licenses, but as the actual products that they are. From my understanding, there are already consumer protection laws that touch on these matters, but no clear statement was ever made that pertained to video games in particular. As such, I believe the Digital Fairness Act would be the perfect place to add a more distinct ruling towards video games in particular. Thank you for your time in reading and considering my feedback.
